message.content: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol involves adversaries stealing data by using a different protocol than the one used for command and control (C2) communications. This technique can involve protocols such as FTP, SMTP, HTTP/S, DNS, SMB, or even Web services like cloud storage. Adversaries may also encrypt or obfuscate these channels to avoid detection. Common methods include using operating system utilities like Net/SMB or FTP, and tools like curl on macOS and Linux. Notable examples include Bundlore using curl to send data to a URL, Chaes using MIME protocol, FrameworkPOS employing DNS tunneling, and TeamTNT using cURL to send files to C2 servers. The impact of this technique is significant as it allows adversaries to bypass traditional network defenses and exfiltrate sensitive data. Mitigation strategies include monitoring network traffic for unusual protocol usage, implementing data loss prevention (DLP) solutions, and employing network segmentation to limit data flow paths. Regularly updating and patching systems can also help prevent exploitation through known vulnerabilities.
